summary: Equity is the backbone of long-term wealth creation, but should you invest directly in stocks, or through mutual funds? 🤔 In this episode of This or That, Bhartendra Singh speaks with Sneha Jain, CEO of Wealth Trust, to simplify equity investing, bust myths around SIPs, and give you a clear roadmap for building wealth.

disclaimer: This podcast is for educational purposes only. It should not be considered financial advice. Please consult a SEBI-registered investment advisor before making any investment decisions.

seo.title: Stocks vs Mutual Funds: The Truth About SIP & Equity Investing | Sneha Jain
seo.description: Equity is the backbone of long-term wealth creation, but should you invest directly in stocks, or through mutual funds? 🤔 In this episode of This or

takeaways: Equity is treated as the backbone of long-term wealth, so the real question is not whether to own it but how: directly through stocks or through mutual funds.
SIP is not the investment. It is only the method you use, so what actually matters is the underlying fund you are putting money into.
Direct stocks come with real risks, costs, and a need for discipline, and evaluating a company means looking at promoters, debt, ROE/ROCE, and PE rather than chasing IPOs or tips.
The episode's portfolio idea leans on mutual funds for the bulk of your equity, roughly 60 to 65 percent, with direct stocks playing a smaller role.
Most investors are told to avoid futures and options and to watch for recency bias, like piling into small-caps after a surge.

fork.decision: Should you build your equity through direct stocks or through mutual funds?

this.label: Direct stocks
points: Start with large-caps and judge a company on promoters, debt, ROE/ROCE, and PE, not on IPO buzz or tips.
Think like an owner and focus on company quality across cycles rather than short-term trading thrill.
It demands more time, discipline, and cost tolerance, which the episode is candid about.

that.label: Mutual funds
points: Simplify equity across index, flexicap, multicap, thematic, and hybrid without picking single stocks.
Build a large, mid, and small-cap mix, then use a core-satellite approach for thematic or global funds.
Lets you diversify and declutter instead of managing a crowded, self-built stock portfolio.

fork.pick: Make mutual funds the base of your equity, around 60 to 65 percent, and keep direct stocks as a smaller, disciplined satellite you actually have time to research. And whichever you choose, remember the SIP is just the pipe: know the underlying fund before you commit.
